content:
{
  "title": "Self-Diagnostic Prompting: Enabling LLMs to Detect and Correct Their Own Mathematical Reasoning Errors",
  "problem": "Current LLMs often make subtle logical or calculation errors in multi-step mathematical reasoning, leading to incorrect solutions even when their initial approach is promising. Existing prompting methods do not reliably help models identify and fix their own errors during problem solving.",
  "motivation": "Improving mathematical problem solving in LLMs is critical for applications in education, automation, and scientific discovery. Recent advances in tool use and chain-of-thought have improved accuracy, but models still lack self-awareness of their reasoning quality. Enabling LLMs to self-diagnose errors promises more robust, trustworthy outputs, especially as these models are increasingly deployed in high-stakes domains.",
  "method": "We propose a two-phase self-diagnostic prompting approach: First, generate an initial solution using chain-of-thought prompting. Second, prompt the model to explicitly review each reasoning step, highlighting potential errors or uncertainties and proposing corrections if needed. This review prompt is designed to encourage metacognitive reflection, leveraging few-shot examples where the model identifies and corrects its own past mistakes. The method can be further enhanced by iteratively repeating the review-correction phase until the solution stabilizes.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate the method on math reasoning benchmarks such as GSM8K and MATH, comparing to standard chain-of-thought and verification-style prompting baselines. Metrics will include solution accuracy, error detection rate, and correction success. Ablation studies will investigate the impact of iterative reviews and the quality of self-diagnosis prompts."
}
